summary: Russia's 2019 law mandating pre-installed government-approved apps on smartphones went into effect, and Apple found a clever compliance workaround: rather than pre-installing apps, they added a setup screen offering the apps for optional download. Users can dismiss the entire list with just two taps. Gruber then pivots to a sharper point: Apple's compliance screens explain that Russian law requires the app suggestions, yet Apple forbids developers from similarly explaining App Store rules to their own users. This double standard on transparency undermines Apple's moral authority on the issue. The juxtaposition reveals that Russia's 'law against Apple' ironically permits more user transparency than Apple's own policies.
key_points: Apple found a compliant solution to Russia's pre-installation law without actually pre-installing any apps, using an optional download screen during device setup
The implementation is deliberately minimal — users can skip all suggested apps with just two taps, and there's no 'Install All' option
Apple uses transparency language ('In compliance with Russian legal requirements') to explain its actions to users, but forbids developers from doing the same about App Store rules
Facebook was rejected for telling users 'Apple takes 30% of this purchase' — the exact kind of transparency Apple claims to value
The core hypocrisy is that Apple's own App Store rules are less transparent to users than what a Russian government mandate requires
money_quotes: In Russia, the law was known as the 'Law Against Apple'.
Effectively, if you don't want any of these apps, the new setup process simply requires two additional taps: 'Continue' on the first screen and 'X' on the second.
It's still unclear what Apple would have done if the Russian government had mandated that these apps actually be preinstalled on every new iPhone.
Apple leverages transparency when it suits them but doesn't let the developers do the same.
It is prima facie wrong that one of the App Store rules is that apps are not allowed to explain the App Store rules to users.
It's quite a thing that Russia's 'law against Apple' allows for more transparency to users than Apple's own App Store rules.
key_insight: Apple's willingness to transparently explain Russian legal mandates to users while simultaneously forbidding developers from explaining App Store rules exposes a fundamental hypocrisy in Apple's claimed commitment to transparency.
